Issue No.9 • April 2012
''Old School'' Telephone Selling
Techniques For Exploding
Your Entail Response
Dear Subscriber,
A while back in the hallowed pages of this letter I mentioned a true telephone sales
jeenius named Stan Billue -- who has been called the most copied, respected, and
referred-to sales trainer alive. One of his (many) claims to sales fame is doubling his own
income each year for five consecutive years selling over the phone. Stan has taught more 6
and 7 figure a year income earning mega-buck sales pros than any other trainer. (In fact, a
little birdy told me guys like Tom Hopkins and Zig Ziglar use his training materials when
selling seats to their own seminars!) What does this have to do with your email?
Everything. Because there are a LOT of things Stan teaches about selling over
the phone that are directly applicable to sellin1 in email. And we're gonna
cover one these techniques in detail (along with examples) so you can start beefing up
your sales in your email marketing, too.
I've been wanting to write about this ever since launching "Email Players."
When I first heard Stan teach I was blown away by what he had to say, and started
applying his ideas to my email campaigns. This is real world stuff I've used over and over

and over. Anyway, while there are dozens of things Stan teaches I've profited from in

... he once told the story about when he was selling high ticket
investments and he called a doctor who was so angry and skeptical he hung up
on Stan as soon as he heard the word "investment." So Stan calls him right
back and says, 	"I'm sorry, 	I 	think we got disconnected •.. " and continued on
(an old school telephone technique) . But the good doctor stopped him in his
tracks again, 	"no you little S.O.B. 	we didn't get disconnected, 	I hung up on
you and if you ever call back I'm going to report you to blah blah blah ... "
and slammed the phone down again. 	Long story short, 	Stan called back a few
more times only to get a busy signal. 	But about a half hour later, 	Stan got
through again.
The doctor angrily picked up the phone, 	"Yeah!!?"
"Doc, 	before you hang up on me a third time I 	have to ask you one more
thing!"
"What?"
"You must have had a HORRIBLE experience with investment salesmen ... "
And from there, 	the guy talked and talked and talked •.• 	until, 	after 15
minutes, 	a sale for over $200k was made. 	Okay, 	there's a lot more to this

There are more details 	I'm leaving out on how he closed the sale 	(it's a

and USE it, 	you'll never have trouble getting your emails opened and read
ever again. 	As Stan says ... 	"Always remember ... 	curiosity always overpowers
programming. He was programmed to blow me off the phone again but he couldn't
get the words out of his mouth." 	In other words .•• 	when he called the angry
guy back and simply said, 	"I have to ask you one more thing!" the immediate
reaction was, 	"What?" -- where Stan now had complete control of the call.
Yes, curiosity gives you control. 	With email this 	is even MORE important.
Everyone is programmed to delete emails on sight, 	right? Especially emails
that look like marketing. 	But if you embed curiosity into your emails -- in
the subject lines, 	opening sentences, body copy and your offers ... you almost
cannot lose.

want to delete your emails on sight. Ways I have used (and continue to use) in my own

email marketing. Below are 5 of the most consistently reliable ways I've used. I challenge

Defile The "Sacred Cows"
This is one of my favorite ways to override programming that sometimes gets me
in trouble (but always in a way I find amusing). This is where you look for the
heroes and beliefs your m.arket holds dear... their "sacred cows" ... and
SKEWER them.! By that I mean, you tell them why those sacred cows are wrong and
maybe even dangerous to your prospect. You have to know your facts solid, of course.
And you don't want to do this lightly. But when you do it right (and telegraph it in the
subject line) it is VERY hard for your list to resist reading. To drive this point home, I'm
going to give you two examples of how I've done this. The first sacred cow I BBQ;d below
is the popular persuasion expert and author of "Influence: The Psychology Of
Persuasion" Robert Cialidini. I actually like RC, don't get me wrong. But at the same
time, there are people in my market who practically idolize him and quote his book
chapter by chapter and verse by verse. So I knew defiling this sacred cow would work to
get lots of attention. And, if I did my job right, create outrage! (Hey, if it ain't
controversial, why bother writing about it?) Anyway, here's the email, try to use it as a
"template" for defiling one of the sacred cows in your market Gust swap out my sacred
cow for yours ... )
Subject Line: The law of narcissocity
Ben,
The "the law of reciprocity" 	amuses me.
At least, 	the way it's popularly taught.
This is something Robert Cialdini wrote in his book, 	"Influence:
The Psychology Of Persuasion." And if you ask some people, 	you'd
almost think this "law" 	is as reliable and predictable as •••
gravity.
Yeah, 	I 	kind of don't think so.
Let me 	'splaineth why.
Basically, 	the 	"law" of reciprocity says when you give someone

something, 	they will feel socially, professionally, 	maybe even

and leads will be more likely to buy out of sheer obligation.
I won't say this never happens.
But, 	it doesn't happen nearly as much as people think.
And, 	even then, 	it's a shoddy foundation to build a business on.
Why?
Well, 	think of it this way:
Is it a GOOD thing for someone to buy not because they actually
want your product ••• 	but because they feel obligated?
Is that going to be a repeat buyer?
A raving testimonial?
Someone who tells their friends about you?
I 	kinda doubt it.
Plus let's face it, 	this is assuming people even feel a sense of
reciprocity in these narcissistic times, 	where they are FAR more
likely to feel a sense of entitlement than obligation.
Finally, 	a purchase might make people buyers.
But they sure ain't customers.
Buyers buy something ••• 	once.
Maybe even twice.
But customers?
Customers make a 	"custom" of buying from you again and again and
again -- because you are serving them, 	giving them what they want,
and offering them a great experience.
You don't build a real business on buyers.

Recruit Celebrities
(Without Their Knowledge)
There are many ways to profit from the existence of celebrities. It's almost
tempting to think maybe God invented celebrities just to help us marketers sell more stuff!
Case in point: Are there any celebrities you can give advice to? How about any celebrities
who suffer from the same problems your product solves? If so, you can usc it to your
advantage and make it tough for readers to ignore what you write about. People love
celebrities (celebrity gossip sells BIG-- just look at the supermarket check out stands
selling gossip rags for prooQ. Here is an example:
Subject line: Tough guy celebrities whimpering in prostate pain
Hi,
Prostate pain is the ultimate equalizer in men.
Nobody is immune to prostate problems, 	and the pain can
drive even the toughest man to his knees, 	begging his doctor
for help.
Even many famous people have had prostate issues.
Some of them include:
Hollywood actors like Robert DiNiro and Roger Moore ••• 	politicians
like Rudy Giuliani and John Kerry •.• 	pro athletes like Joe Torre
and Arnold Palmer ••• 	and even retired Four-Star Army General Norman
Schwarzkopf, 	Nelson Mandela and singer Harry Belfonte 	(not my
favorite person, 	but I would not wish prostate pain on him or
anyone else) •

This is related to defiling the sacred cows of your market. Except, you're not

other common and taken-for-granted information out there you disagree with. It's very
tough for people to ignore facts that run counter to what the snooty experts say. Some
people will cheer you on and others will have to read just because they can't resist seeing
how you came to the conclusion you're making. Here is an example:

selenium did they use, 	and how much?

But not all supplements are created equal.

I'm sure there are studies or so-called "facts" in your market you can debunk like
this (every market has them, even if you have to root around and look for them). And
when you do, you can be almost certain your emails will get opened, read and probably
even purchased from a lot more readily.
Selling Against Type
Whenever you can take something your market takes at face value, without
question, and turn it on its head -- you have a recipe for an email that is bound to get
opened 2-3 times more than anything else. This is something many of the great sales and
copywriting masters have known for decades (yet how many books/courses/seminars
actually teach it?). The key is to find something your market has their personalities
wrapped up in and believing to the point where it's almost silly for you to counter it. But
if you do your job right and have proven yourself to be a reliable source of
good infonnation, they alm.ost won't be able to help but open and read your
eDlail! Plus, if you make a good enough case for yourself (again, have your facts air tight
before doing this!), you will likely persuade many more people to buy your product than
you would normally. Here's an example that helped us sell a lot of weight loss eBooks:
Subject Line: The case against exercise!
So I'm going to take some "heat" for this.
But I 	think one of the most useless things you can do for dropping
weight 	(including belly fatl) 	is exercise.
But waitl
Before anyone sics the lions on me, 	let me explain •••
I'm not saying exercise is bad.
I'm just saying as a way to lose size and fat, 	it's really
overrated and won't do you nearly as much good as learning how to
eat right. And by eat right I 	don't mean munching on carrot sticks
or chewing cud like a rabbitl
I'm just saying exercise is not really this big fat burner.
At least, 	not like most believe it is.
At most, 	you might burn a couple hundred calories.

One of the all-time great direct mail copywriters is a fellow named jim Rutz. He is
not someone who calls attention to himself. And so he doesn't have the "rock star"
positioning of say, a john Carlton or even a Gary Bencivenga. But rest assured,Jim Rutz
is pretty much universally acknowledged as one of the top 3 direct mail copywriters on
the planet. And a while back I had the opportunity to read an interview where he
explained some really cool copywriting secrets I had never heard before. One of which
was his "ghost story" secret. To illustrate his point, he told a story about a kindergarten
teacher who was having trouble keeping her students quiet and still during story time. In
fact, they were always talking and squirming around and would not sit still... except ...
when she told a story about ghosts! It's like as soon as she added a ghost to the story (no
matter what the story was about) they'd sit still and listen intently to every word she said.
Andjim Rutz's point was you have to figure out what your market's "ghost story" is.
What is it that they HAVE to know more about or find so interesting and fascinating they
can't help but sit still, ignore everything else around them, and keep their eyes glued to
your message. That, my friend, is a great way to overcome the programming your readers
have to delete your emails on sight. This takes some research but once you find out what
your readers' "ghost stories" are you can work it in to your emails regularly and see lots of
great results. For example, in weight loss I discovered a hot button for the market is when
their FaceBook friends would tag their photos of when they are overweight. (Oh, and by
the way, I have a very cool "short cut" way of finding out EXACTLY what your market's
hot buttons are I'll be showing you soon -- this secret alone has made it so I can crank out
emails in less than 5 minutes most of the time to the weight loss niche.) Where was I? Oh
yes ... heavy FaceBook photos are humiliating for them. And it REALLY pisses them off
(hey, who can blame them?) So since learning that, I sometimes work something about
FaceBook photos or drivers license photos (another hot button) into emails whenever it's
relevant and makes sense. Here's an example of how I did it recently in an extremely
successful email that nabbed us around 1WICE as many sales as we usually get on any
given day:
Subject: 	Friends tagging your fat pictures in FaceBook
Hey hey!
Everyone has their tipping point.
By that I mean, 	that one event that happens where you decide
"that's enough! 	I'm getting rid of this fat once and forever!"
It's different for everyone.

Anyway, there are MANY more ways to use curiosity to override your reader's
natural inclination to delete emails on sight. But these 5 ways should be enough to get
some more$$ in your hot little hand. In fact, right now I challenge YOU to write an
email for each of these 5 ways (5 emails) and send one out per day over the next 5 days.
Keep track of your sales. I don't have a crystal ball, but if you do it right and know your
market well enough (and if you don't, make learning your market's hot buttons priority
numero UNO) ... then methinks you'll see a healthy bump in sales.
How To Have ''Power Positioning''
There's one more Stan Billue "trick" I want to share before we close out this
month's issue. One paradox about Stan Billue is, he may be the world's greatest telephone
sales man ... but he HATES rejection. (Who doesn't?). So that's why when he would cold
call corporate sales managers to sell his "How To Double Your Income Selling Over The
Phone" course he would not personally make the calls. Well, maybe "technically" he
made the calls (as far as physically picking the phone up, dialing and talking). But it
wasn't Stan Billue who was calling-- it was his alter ego "Pat Mu:phy." In
other words, he pretended to be a spokesman (named Pat Murphy) calling on Stan
Billue's behalf. Sheer brilliance! And he granted himself instant soHd
marketplace positioning in the process. There's something about a 3rd party doing
this sort of thing that makes you far more valuable in the other person's eyes. Like you're
so important and busy you can't be bothered to waste your time. Certain very wealthy
people in MLM do this. They have someone else call their leads to set their appointments.
When they get on the phone they have almost like "super positioning" and whatever
message you want to convey gets more attention and is taken more seriously.
OK, so what's this got to do with email?
Well, there's no reason why your outgoing emails when contacting potential joint
venture partners or even customers have to be "from" you. They can be "from" anyone
you want -- your assistant, secretary, affiliate manager, or whoever you like. I have a friend
and "Email Players" subscriber (who SHALT remain nameless here for obvious
reasons ... ) who does this. Even his customer service is not "him"-- it's his so-
called "assistant." This makes him far more valuable in other peoples' eyes and gives
him a unique kind of marketplace positioning he would NEVER have using his own
name for those kinds of communications.
